data.title: Vacancy Rate Across Auckland's Central Business District Rises to Nearly 14% in H2 2024
data.description:
The vacancy rate across Auckland's central business district rose to almost 14% in the six months to December 2024 from a little over 13% in the prior half-year period, according to a study by Colliers International published March 6.

Prime-grade vacancy, which is a combination of A-grade and premium space, grew to 9.8% from 7.5%. Tenants such as BNZ and Beca took advantage of new developments, which allowed them to "move to higher grade space, relocate to a favored precinct, and to optimize their office footprint," per the report.

Tenants continued to prefer top-quality space amid business efforts to entice employees back into the office. Features such as office floors with an existing fit-out were particularly popular among prospective tenants, per the report.

"Being able to lease space such as this holds appeal for firms because they can quickly establish their operations and avoid extensive costs when relocating," said Rob Bird, national director of office leasing at Colliers.

data.title: Dreadnought Resources' Fiscal H1 Loss Widens
data.description:
Dreadnought Resources reported that loss per diluted share in the fiscal first half widened to AU$0.0038 from AU$0.001 a year earlier, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

Interest income for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$2,455, down from AU$115,727 in the same period a year earlier.

data.title: Income Asset Management Enters Mediation With BGC Partners Over Legal Dispute
data.description:
Income Asset Management Group moved into confidential mediation as part of its ongoing legal proceedings with BGC Partners (BGC), trading as Fixed Income Solutions, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

The lawsuit alleges that two of IAM's employees breached restraint of trade provisions in their previous employment contracts with BGC, the filing said.

IAM is accused of inducing these breaches, and BGC is seeking damages and costs, the filing added.

The company denies the claims and is vigorously defending the case, continuing to incur legal expenses while its insurer reviews the policy coverage, per the filing.

In the same filing, IAM said it transitioned nearly AU$1.8 billion of client bond and loan assets to Perpetual Corporate Trust, expected to boost its annual financial performance by AU$600,000 annually from fiscal 2026.

data.title: Fed's Daly said growing uncertainty among businesses could slow demand in the US economy
data.description:
Mary Daly, President of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, stated that rising uncertainty among businesses could dampen demand in the U.S. economy but does not justify a change in interest rates.

  • business leaders in her district report heightened uncertainty about the economy and policy, which research suggests can reduce demand
  • Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) does not need to adjust rates when it meets next week
  • believes current interest rates are appropriate
  • believes the economy remains strong

Instead of reacting to short-term news or market fluctuations, she advocates for a more measured approach to evaluating economic conditions.Her remarks align with comments from Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who also stated on Friday that policymakers do not need to rush any changes despite growing uncertainty.

  • Fed's Powell: U.S. central bank does not need to be in a hurry to adjust interest rates
  • Q&A from Fed's Powell: No need to redefine price stability

--

Mary Daly

Daly's remarks from her LinkedIn post on Friday.

data.title: St Barbara Expects 2H Gold Production to Be Weighted Toward 4Q
St Barbara Previously Expected FY Simberi Production of 65,000-75,000 Oz
St Barbara Raises FY Simberi Capital Expenditure Guidance
St Barbara Previously Expected FY Simberi AISC of A$3,200-A$3,600/Oz
St Barbara Lowers FY Simberi Sustaining Capex to A$5M-A$10M
St Barbara Expects FY Simberi Growth Capex of A$40M-A$50M
Weekend - Lutnick says Trump sticking to US aluminum-steel tariff timeline
data.description:
U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick indicated that the 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, set to take effect on Wednesday, are unlikely to be postponed.

  • When asked on NBC’s Meet the Press over the weekend if the tariffs would go into effect on March 12 as planned, Lutnick confirmed, “Yes.”

The tariffs, imposed by President Donald Trump in February, cover imports from key suppliers like Canada and Mexico and extend to finished metal products. While U.S. steelmakers have urged Trump to maintain the levies without exemptions, the measures could impact American businesses that rely on these materials.

Officials say the policy aims to curb efforts by countries such as Russia and China to circumvent existing trade restrictions.

---

Trump’s approach to tariffs—often characterized by abrupt announcements, reversals, and delays—has created uncertainty and market volatility.

data.title: St Barbara Expects 2H Simberi Production of 32,500-42,500 Oz
St Barbara Expects 2H Simberi All-In Sustaining Cost of A$3,400-A$3,800/Oz
St Barbara: Simberi Did Not Achieve Targeted Face Positions in Two Key Mining Locations
Star Entertainment Group to be Removed From ASX 200 Index
data.description: Star Entertainment Group will be removed from the ASX 200 index before the opening of trading on March 24 as a result of S&P Down Jones Indices' quarterly review, according to a March 7 Australian bourse filing.

data.title: St Barbara: Miss Resulted in Lower Than Anticipated Average Mined Grade
St Barbara Expects FY Simberi Production of 55,000-65,000 Oz
St Barbara Expects FY Simberi AISC of A$3,900-A$4,200/Oz
Oil producers starting to hold back capital - "the opposite of what the president wants"
data.description:
A few items via Reuters over the weekend ahead of Ceraweek, where energy industry leaders meet in Houston.

Josh Young, chief investment officer at Bison Interests.:

  • "The costs are way higher and that affects the profitability,
  • "You're starting to see producers hold back their capital. It's the opposite of what the president wants."

Dan Pickering, chief investment officer at Pickering Energy Partners.

  • "Shareholders are saying capital discipline, return cash to shareholders and then you have the most powerful man in the world, saying, drill, baby, drill - I think you pay lip service to the president, and you follow the wishes of the shareholder"

What is it they say? The best cure for low oil prices is low oil prices.

  • ie - when the cycle sees job cuts, capex pulled back etc then that'll lead to tighter supply and higher prices. The key idea to keep in mind is 'eventually', K?

---

CERAWeek is an annual energy conference organized by S&P Global

  • brings together global leaders, policymakers, executives, and experts from the energy, technology, and financial sectors
  • CERAWeek 2025 is scheduled for March 10-14

data.title: Myer Holdings to Join S&P/ASX 300 Index
data.description: Myer Holdings will join the S&P/ASX 300 Index prior to market open on March 24, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

data.title: Australia shares set to rise at open; NZ slightly up
data.description:
Australian shares were poised to rise on Monday, with probable gains in commodity stocks, while investors continue to navigate global market uncertainty amid the ongoing trade war between major economies.

The local share price index futures (YAPcm1) rose 0.9%, a 62.8-point premium to the underlying S&P/ASX 200 index close. The benchmark fell 1.8% to close below the psychologically crucial 8,000 mark on Friday.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index rose 0.1% in early trading.

data.title: Astral Resources Extends Takeover Offer for Maximus Resources
data.description: Astral Resources NL extended its off-market takeover offer for Maximus Resources to March 21 from the original deadline of March 7, according to a filing with the Australian Securities Exchange on March 7.

data.title: New Frontier Minerals Says Sale Of Broken Hill East Project To Impact Minerals
data.description:
Impact Minerals Ltd :

  • SALE OF BROKEN HILL EAST PROJECT TO IMPACT MINERALS

data.title: Jervois Global Shareholders Call for General Meeting
data.description:
Jervois Global received two notices under section 249D of the Corporations Act from shareholders holding at least 5% of the company's votes, according to a March 7, filing with the Australian bourse.

The notices request the company to call a general meeting to consider passing a special resolution to amend the company's constitution and resolutions to remove certain directors, the filing said.

Jervois is reviewing the validity of the notices and will determine its next steps in accordance with the Corporations Act, the filing added.

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Inflation Data, Bank of Canada Decision Due
data.description:
U.S. inflation data for February will be the highlight among economic data as investors continue to follow the back-and-forth of U.S. President Trump's announcements on tariffs.

A rate decision by the Bank of Canada is due, where rates are likely to be cut amid concern about the economic impact of U.S. tariffs on Canadian imports.

In Asia, the focus is on China's annual legislature session, which will end during the week, as well as a slew of data releases from Japan.

U.S.

As more headlines from President Trump on tariffs and Ukraine will continue to be closely monitored, Wednesday's U.S. inflation data for February will be the highlight of the week's economic data.

The U.S. Federal Reserve has raised concerns recently that inflation isn't slowing as much as it had hoped. The data could continue to reflect this, particularly given evidence that the prospect of tariffs has already been feeding through into higher prices.

Business surveys suggest that some companies are pre-empting tariffs by lifting prices, ING economists said in a note. Food prices have also been rising, while energy prices could additionally contribute to more inflation, they said.

Still, the impact of the data on U.S. rate expectations could be limited because of growing concerns about the impact of Trump's policies, especially tariffs, on the economy.

"The market appears more focused on worries about growth right now in an environment of government austerity and the threat of a squeeze on spending power if prices do respond to tariffs," ING said.

U.S. money markets are now pricing in three 25 basis-point rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve this year, versus just one only a few weeks ago.

"A 0.3% month-on-month inflation print is unlikely to change that situation."

With doubts starting to grow about the strength of the U.S. economy, the University of Michigan March preliminary consumer survey on Friday, March 14 could also be significant for gauging sentiment amid tariff uncertainty and public-sector job losses.

Other data include February producer price data and weekly jobless claims figures on Thursday.

On tariffs, the U.S. is set to impose steel and aluminium tariffs of 25% on the EU from Wednesday.

The Treasury will auction $58 billion in three-year notes on Tuesday, $39 billion in 10-year notes on Wednesday and $22 billion in 30-year bonds on Thursday.

CANADA

Canada is one of U.S. President Trump's key targets for tariffs, putting Canadian data and events more in the spotlight than usual.

The Bank of Canada announces a rate decision on Wednesday. With Canada's jobs data for February coming in weak, even before tariffs were implemented, a rate cut looks increasingly likely.

"The slowdown in hiring even before tariffs were implemented gives the BOC an easy pass to cut interest rates another quarter point," economists at Desjardins Group said in a note.

Money markets are pricing in a 78% chance of a rate cut at the upcoming meeting, LSEG data show.

EUROZONE

The data calendar in the coming week is slim, leaving investors continuing to ponder the implications of Germany's large-scale fiscal stimulus plans--which caused a steep rise in German government-bond yields--as well as a planned step-up in defense spending on the continent.

Investors are particularly interested in how the fiscal expansion will impact Europe's growth and inflation, and how that could impact the European Central Bank's monetary policy.

Vanguard and AllianceBernstein already expect fewer ECB rate cuts after this month's 25-basis-point reduction in the deposit rate to 2.50%. Vanguard now expects only one more rate cut this year, while AllianceBernstein's revised baseline for the ECB's policy rate at the end of 2025 is 2%, up from a previous expectation of 1.75%.

Industrial production data from Germany for January are due Monday, followed by eurozone industrial production figures Thursday. Final February inflation data from France and Spain are due on Friday, March 14.

Germany will auction 4.5 billion euros in March 2027 Schatz on Tuesday and 4.5 billion euros in February 2035 Bund on Wednesday. Portugal holds an auction on Wednesday and Italy on Thursday.

U.K.

U.K. gross domestic product data for January are due Friday, March 14 and come after unexpectedly strong GDP for December.

Investec economist Philip Shaw said the data could continue to be solid, but there are many uncertainties ahead which could weigh on growth going forward.

"We are forecasting a 0.2% increase in GDP on the month, but would emphasise that this represents a brisker pace of expansion than is likely over the remainder of the year," he said.

The U.K. plans to launch a new September 2049 index-linked gilt via syndication in the week starting on March 10, subject to demand and market conditions. The U.K. plans the sale of March 2035 gilt Wednesday.

POLAND

Poland's central bank announces a rate decision on Wednesday. It is expected to leave its key interest rate on hold at 5.75% due to continued concerns about inflation.

SCANDINAVIA, SWITZERLAND

Sweden will hold a bond auction on Wednesday. Switzerland will hold a bond auction on Wednesday.

CHINA

China's annual legislature session will end Tuesday when lawmakers are expected to approve a government work report that includes an ambitious 2025 growth target of around 5% for the world's second-largest economy.

Attention is on whether Beijing will follow up on stimulus pledges with more concrete measures, with senior officials having announced plans to step up fiscal spending and boost consumption.

Next week, China's central bank will likely release its gauges of the country's credit growth for February. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal estimated that banks issued 1.23 trillion yuan of new loans last month, a significant fall from January's 5.13 trillion yuan, mainly due to seasonality. Meanwhile, M2, a broad gauge of money supply, may have expanded 7% in February from a year earlier, matching the pace seen in January.

JAPAN

The Japanese Trade Union Confederation, known as Rengo, is scheduled to announce preliminary results for annual spring wage negotiations on Friday, March 14. Japan's largest trade union group has said its member unions are seeking an average wage increase of 6.09%, the biggest request in three decades. These wage negotiations are closely watched by economists and investors for clues on the timing of future Bank of Japan interest-rate increases.

Revised gross domestic product data, scheduled to be released Tuesday, is likely to confirm that the Japanese economy expanded for the third-straight quarter during the October-December period. The preliminary report released in February showed that real GDP grew an annualized 2.8% in the final quarter of 2024.

Separate government data due Tuesday may also signal a recovery in private consumption. Household spending is expected to have increased 3.5% in January from a year earlier, according to a poll of economists by data provider Quick. That compares with the 2.7% rise seen in December.

Figures on current account balance for January and bank lending for February are also slated for release Monday.

The Ministry of Finance is scheduled to auction 2.3 trillion yen of five-year sovereign notes on Monday and 1 trillion yen of 20-year JGBs on Wednesday. The 20-year JGBs to be auctioned will be a reopening of the January 2025 issue, the ministry said. Investors such as insurance companies and pension funds are likely to show more bidding interest at the 20-year auction owing to the issue's higher yields that will probably be available.

AUSTRALIA

On Tuesday, National Australia Bank's business survey for February will attract some attention as markets weigh whether global events might be starting to dent confidence.

Australia's economy has been supported by government spending over the last year, with private demand subdued. The NAB survey may offer some clues on a private demand recovery, which will help to offset an expected slide in public demand later this year.

INDIA

India's February inflation print on Wednesday will likely show a further cooling in price pressures, which could pave the way for further monetary policy easing.

Bank of America economist Rahul Bajoria forecasts headline inflation to have eased to 4.0%, the midpoint of the central bank's target range, which would mark the slowest price growth in six months. Food and vegetable prices likely eased during the month, Bajoria said.

The Reserve Bank of India sees food inflation pressures softening in the coming months, but has warned that continued volatility in energy prices and adverse weather conditions pose upside risks to prices.

Given recent data showing a slowdown in growth and easing inflation, ANZ's Dhiraj Nim and Sanjay Mathur see a "case for a stronger policy response" by the central bank. The economists said in a note that the RBI could do more to stimulate the economy, and expect the RBI to deliver at least two more rate cuts by June. ANZ pegs India's inflation at 3.8% for February.

  • Any references to days are in local times.

Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Hoi Shan Chan at hoishan.chan@wsj.com

data.title: Weekend: China's housing minister said government to promote purchase of existing housing
data.description:
China's housing minister, Ni Hong, spoke on Sunday

  • said that the country's property market is showing signs of improvement
  • growing confidence among buyers
  • market continued its positive trajectory in January and February, stabilizing after previous declines
  • the government plans to encourage the purchase of existing homes and accelerate urban village redevelopment
  • government will accelerate lending for 'While List' projects
  • government will give more autonomy to local governments for affordable housing purchases
  • local government special bonds to be used for purchasing idle land and housing stock

data.title: Here’s what happened in crypto today
data.description:
Today in crypto, disappointment surrounding the US Strategic Bitcoin Reserve signals unrealistic investor expectations, according to regulatory experts, Michael Saylor advocated for an aggressive US government approach to Bitcoin accumulation, and the Bitcoin community shared mixed reactions to the White House Crypto Summit.

Bitcoin reserve backlash signals unrealistic industry expectations

The widespread disappointment surrounding the US Strategic Bitcoin Reserve — hailed as a historic step for Bitcoin adoption — suggests unrealistic investor expectations, according to regulatory experts.

President Donald Trump signed an executive order on March 7, which will utilize Bitcoin (BTC) seized in government criminal cases rather than purchasing the asset directly from the market. The announcement triggered a more than 6% drop in Bitcoin’s price, falling from $90,400 to $84,979, according to Cointelegraph Markets Pro data.

The reaction signals unrealistic industry expectations, according to Anastasija Plotnikova, co-founder and CEO of Fideum, a regulatory and blockchain infrastructure firm focused on institutions.

“It was very clear that the US government could utilize the existing BTC in their possession, aka seized funds,” she told Cointelegraph, adding:

“It is bizarre to see such a big public disappointment coming from some industry players. [...] Not that long ago, even the idea of BTC Reserve held and supported by a federal government was a revolutionary idea, and now we see a very solid implementation.”

The Bitcoin reserve is a “cautious” approach with taxpayer funds, which “make this decision well aligned with the messaging from this administration,” added the regulatory expert.

Michael Saylor pushes US gov’t to purchase up to 25% of Bitcoin supply

Strategy founder Michael Saylor has proposed that the United States government aims to acquire up to 25% of Bitcoin’s total supply over the next decade for its Strategic Bitcoin Reserve.

“Acquire 5-25% of the Bitcoin network in trust for the nation through consistent, programmatic daily purchases between 2025 and 2035, when 99% of all BTC will have been issued,” Saylor wrote in a document titled “A Digital Assets Strategy to Dominate the 21st Century Global Economy.” 

Saylor presented the document to US President Donald Trump, government executives, and global crypto leaders at the White House Crypto Summit on March 7.

He explained that the government should stick to a “Never sell your Bitcoin” policy, predicting that by 2045, the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve could generate over $10 trillion annually, and serve as a “perpetual source of prosperity” for Americans.

Bitcoin community has mixed reactions to White House Summit

The Bitcoin (BTC) community voiced mixed reactions to the recent White House Crypto Summit, with some calling the event underwhelming and others characterizing the summit as a historic milestone for digital assets.

Retail investors and Bitcoin maximalists tended to have a more critical view of the Trump administration’s first White House Crypto Summit.

“The White House’ crypto summit’ is a gathering of rent-seeking lobbyists pushing state-approved surveillance tokens,” BTC maximalist Justin Bechler wrote on X.

However, institutional investors and asset managers viewed the meeting much more positively and hailed it as a massive success.

Kyle Samani, the managing partner at Multicoin Capital, attended the summit — characterizing it as a “historic moment” for the digital asset industry. “We used to be fighting to stay alive. Now, we forge the path to lead the world in crypto,” Samani wrote.

data.title: Spooked by tariffs, funds purge bullish corn bets in near-record fashion: Braun
data.description:
An imminent trade war between the United States and its two largest agricultural trading partners sent bullish Chicago corn speculators running for the hills last week.

But very few bears were made of the ordeal.

On Thursday, U.S. tariffs against most Mexican and Canadian goods were postponed until April. However, the levies had gone into effect on Tuesday and the market reaction was harsh, especially with Mexico the top destination for U.S. corn.

Most-active CBOT corn futures plunged 8.6% in the week ended March 4, their biggest such downturn since mid-2023. Money managers during the week slashed their net long in CBOT corn futures and options to 219,752 contracts from 337,454 a week prior.

Weekly net corn selling near 118,000 contracts was the second-largest ever, behind the 147,000 contracts sold during the week ended February 28, 2023. But last week's reduction in gross longs, which exceeded 100,000 contracts, was by far a record.

On average, funds' biggest net selling weeks in corn are somewhat evenly split between exiting longs and new short positions. An unusually low 11% of last week's move owed to new gross shorts, suggesting that the selloff was more risk-off in nature rather than a genuinely bearish vote.

In the periphery of the week's events, the U.S. Department of Agriculture predicted strong 2025 U.S. corn plantings and thus a recovery in domestic supplies, though that had been largely expected. South American corn crop outlooks have also recently improved.

But accessible global corn supplies are historically thin, especially in Brazil where stocks are the lightest in over two decades. Demand has persevered in the meantime, pushing investors to build their bullish corn stance after forging a record bearish one last year.

SOYBEANS AND WHEAT

Speculators were also hefty net sellers in wheat and the soy complex during the week ended March 4, but unlike in corn, new short positions drove these moves.

Money managers ditched their net long in CBOT soybean futures and options, flipping to a net short of 35,487 contracts versus a net long of 8,209 in the prior week. Funds' bullish stint in beans lasted just seven weeks, and net selling in the latest week was the strongest since last June.

Most-active CBOT soybeans fell nearly 5% through March 4, while soymeal (SMv1) eased 3% and soyoil (BOv1) plunged 7%. Funds erased much of their net long in CBOT soybean oil futures and options, which dropped to 9,669 contracts from 43,052 a week earlier.

Their net short in soybean meal futures and options reached a 10-week high of 85,344 contracts versus 63,193 in the week before, almost entirely the result of new gross shorts.

In CBOT wheat futures and options, the entrance of new gross shorts was the heaviest for any week since 2017. Money managers also added a handful of gross longs, though the net short grew to 82,399 contracts from 67,614 a week earlier.

So far this year, funds' bearish wheat bets and bullish corn bets have been historically out of sync, though last week's epic corn selloff pulled things closer to normal territory.

However, corn futures rallied nearly 4% over the last three sessions with the delay of U.S. tariffs on Mexico and Canada. Other gains were as follows: soybeans 2.6%, wheat 2.7%, soymeal 3.7% and soyoil 1.4%.

This week, the trade will be watching for the U.S. Department of Agriculture's monthly supply and demand report due on Tuesday. Large changes are not anticipated, though the agency could factor tariff impacts into its estimates if deemed necessary.

Karen Braun is a market analyst for Reuters. Views expressed above are her own.

data.title: GOP Government Funding Plan Sets Up Clash With Democrats — WSJ
data.description:
By Katy Stech Ferek

WASHINGTON — Republicans are moving ahead with a proposal endorsed by President Trump that would keep the federal government funded through September while cutting spending, setting up a showdown in the week ahead with Democrats.

The proposal, released Saturday, would keep federal agencies funded until Sept. 30. It lowers the amount of nondefense discretionary spending by $13 billion from the 2024 funding level, while increasing the amount of money for defense spending by about $6 billion. It also boosts funding for border enforcement. The military funding is meant to win over defense hawks who have rejected past short-term measures that they said have underfunded the U.S. military.

House lawmakers are expected to vote on the proposal, termed a continuing resolution or CR, as soon as Tuesday, with a majority needed to pass it. But even if House Republicans manage to stay united and pass the proposal over Democratic opposition, it will need at least 60 votes in the Senate, which has only 53 Republican lawmakers.

Funding for federal agencies runs out on Friday night. With no new legislation, many federal employees would be furloughed, while others would continue to report to work with no pay.

Representatives for House Republican leaders said that they worked closely with Trump in crafting the bill. That coordination could help them avoid an embarrassing scenario in which a large number of Republicans reject the proposal. Earlier this week, House Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.) said that he has enough votes to pass the new measure in the House, despite a tight 218-214 split.

"The House and Senate have put together, under the circumstances, a very good funding bill," Trump said Saturday on Truth Social. "All Republicans should vote (Please!) YES next week," he said, adding in capital letters, "no dissent."

More than a dozen House Republicans have never voted to pass a temporary funding bill under Johnson's leadership, thanks largely to unmet demands for more spending cuts, and he has been forced to rely heavily on Democratic votes to approve such measures in the past. But many of these GOP holdouts are expected to get on board this time, thanks to pressure from Trump and ambitions for passing a Republican bill pairing tax cuts and spending reductions later this year.

"There's overwhelming support for the bill," Rep. Chip Roy (R., Texas) said Sunday on "Fox & Friends Weekend" on Fox News. "There are still a few folks who don't like it."

Rep. Tony Gonzales (R., Texas), who said recently that continuing resolutions are a "rubberstamp of fraud, waste and abuse" and that he would oppose one, said Sunday that he is now undecided on how he will vote.

"I think CRs are terrible, but I also think you need to give the president the benefit of the doubt, especially when his policies are working," he said on CNN's "State of the Union."

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D., N.Y.), who opposes the GOP bill, has said he didn't think that Republicans would be able to come up with enough votes for it to pass.

In a statement with other House Democratic leadership, he said the measure "does nothing to protect Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, while exposing the American people to further pain throughout this fiscal year."

That sentiment is shared by other Democrats, angered by the Trump administration's cuts to federal jobs and government programs.

Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D., Mich.) said Sunday on NBC's "Meet the Press" that she will withhold her vote for the proposal until she sees "some assurances that whatever we pass next week is going to ensure that money is spent the way Congress intends."

The last time Congress faced a shutdown, Trump, as president-elect, spoke out against a bipartisan deal that Johnson had negotiated with Democrats in December, prompting him to kill the measure before lawmakers voted on it. They later approved a slimmed-down version that set the March deadline.

Unlike the December measure, the 99-page proposal released on Saturday doesn't contain funding for pet projects requested by individual lawmakers for their hometowns, something that billionaire Trump cost-cutting czar Elon Musk had criticized.

Annual funding bills for federal agencies, when passed by Congress in previous years, come with lengthy reports — written jointly by Republicans and Democrats — with specific directives on how agency leaders should spend their allocated amount of federal money. The proposal from Johnson on Saturday contained far less guidance, giving Trump administration officials more power to spend the money as they wish.

Democratic leaders have said they want to extend government funding levels by a shorter period, giving lawmakers more time to negotiate a package of funding proposals that contain the bipartisan spending guidance for each agency.

Sen. Patty Murray (D., Wash.), the top Democrat on the chamber's appropriations committee, said Saturday that the proposal would give Trump and Musk the power of the purse that is typically reserved for Congress.

She said Congress should pass a short-term measure to prevent a shutdown and finish work on bipartisan funding bills to "ensure our constituents have a say in how federal funding is spent." The overall amount of money for federal agencies falls below both the fiscal year 2024 funding level and the level set in 2023 in a compromise between former President Joe Biden and former House Speaker Kevin McCarthy (R., Calif.).
